summary: The implementation is generally well-covered and degrades per-pipeline Airflow failures to unknown status. The main residual risks are operational: noncritical startup validation can block service readiness serially across the registry, task-instance history queries are effectively unbounded within MAX_HISTORY_DAYS, and missing DAGs still trigger the full downstream Airflow call chain.

findings.summary: Best-effort startup validation can block application readiness for every registered DAG when Airflow is slow or unreachable.
findings.details: The FastAPI lifespan awaits _run_startup_validation before yielding the app. That validation loops over registry.pipelines serially and calls list_dag_task_ids for each entry. AirflowClient retries timeouts and 5xx errors by default, so an unreachable Airflow can delay startup by roughly registry_size * 3 * 10 seconds before /api/healthz or any pipeline endpoint becomes available. The comments say validation should never fail startup, but this still makes process readiness depend on a noncritical Airflow drift check.
findings.risk: A restart during an Airflow incident or with a large registry can leave the backend unavailable even though its health endpoint and stale/cacheable status behavior could otherwise serve users.
findings.suggested_fix: Move validation after readiness into a background task, add a startup-validation timeout/concurrency limit, or make validation optional/nonblocking. Cache task lists by dag_id if multiple registry entries share a DAG.
findings.test_gap: The integration tests use a fast fake list_dag_task_ids and do not cover slow validation, timeout multiplication, large registries, or health readiness while validation is pending.

findings.summary: Each refresh fetches and parses all task instances in the history window twice per pipeline.
findings.details: get_latest_task_instance and get_latest_successful_task_instance both call _list_task_instances. That helper queries the Airflow batch taskInstances endpoint with only execution_date_gte, then parses every returned row client-side. The code intentionally avoids truncation because the endpoint does not provide server-side ordering or limit, but MAX_HISTORY_DAYS defaults to 30 and permits up to 365. For frequent schedules, one pipeline can return tens or hundreds of thousands of task-instance rows per refresh, twice, every cache TTL.
findings.risk: A minutely DAG over the default 30-day window can produce around 43k rows per task-instance query; over 365 days it can exceed 500k rows. This can overload Airflow's API/database and create high backend memory/latency spikes during snapshot refreshes.
findings.suggested_fix: Use a more bounded retrieval strategy where possible, combine latest and latest-success selection into one history fetch, lower/shape max_history_days by schedule tier, or add explicit hard caps and degraded status when the result set is too large.
findings.test_gap: The regression test covers correctness when the newest row is deep in a 201-row response, but there is no test or benchmark for large payload behavior, duplicate history fetches, memory pressure, or warning-to-degradation behavior.

findings.summary: Missing DAGs do not short-circuit the Airflow state resolver, so a known missing DAG still triggers the rest of the call chain.
findings.details: resolve_airflow_state calls get_dag first, but even when it returns None the resolver continues through active run, latest task instance, latest successful task instance, recent runs, and freshness states. This can turn a simple 'DAG not found' condition into extra upstream traffic and, depending on Airflow endpoint behavior, a later generic AirflowError that obscures the clearer DAG-missing reason. The current unit test explicitly locks in the non-short-circuit behavior.

anchors.excerpt: # All remaining calls must still have been made.

findings.risk: Registry drift or a deleted DAG can cause avoidable Airflow traffic on every refresh. If downstream endpoints respond differently for missing DAGs, the UI may receive a generic fetch-failed reason instead of the more actionable DAG-not-found status reason.
findings.suggested_fix: After get_dag returns None, return a ResolvedAirflowState with dag_info=None and empty downstream fields. Update tests to assert the short-circuit and preserve compute_status_reason's DAG-not-found message.
findings.test_gap: Existing tests assert the opposite behavior and do not verify the end-to-end status reason when a real AirflowClient returns None for get_dag but later endpoints fail.

positive_observations: Per-pipeline snapshot failures are isolated and converted to unknown status instead of failing the whole snapshot.
Configuration validates authentication, HTTPS-by-default for non-local Airflow URLs, bounded recent run limits, and bounded history days.
The cache uses a single-flight lock and retains the previous snapshot after warm-cache refresh failures.
Tests cover status precedence, config validation, registry validation, cache behavior, Airflow retry behavior, and API response envelopes.

commands.result: failed: pytest executable not found from repository root

limitations: This was a local static review plus targeted test execution, not a live Airflow integration test.
No source files outside reviews/ were modified.
The review did not inspect registry data outside the requested airflow_status source tree and tests.
Performance risks were reasoned from code paths and configuration bounds; no load test or memory profile was run.
The code-audit skill and its prescribed artifact format were intentionally not used.
